videos.title: 🇰🇭| How to STAY OUT OF TROUBLE in Phnom Penh CAMBODIA? At the very least, FOLLOW THESE RULES…
videos.description:
Street interviews in Phnom Penh to see how we can stay out of trouble in the capital city of Cambodia.

📍|   This is filmed in Phnom Penh Cambodia, Near the Royal Palace; https://goo.gl/maps/AiZM95HYntQ81umx9

The main point:
- Keep an eye on your belongings at all times. Don't bring any valuables to the beach
- Don’t leave anything near an open window
- Don't get too drunk
- Don't try to rush the locals
- Avoid the riverside after midnight
- When getting a message, just bring the amount you'll need to pay for it. (Don’t bring your phone).

🏥|   SafetyWings Nomad Insurance is Reliable and affordable Travel Insurance; 
https://safetywing.com/nomad-insurance/?referenceID=ChaiTravel&utm_source=ChaiTravel&utm_medium=Ambassador

videos.title: PHNOM PENH, CAMBODIA | 8 Awesome Things To Do In & Around Phnom Penh (for 2-3 day visit)
videos.description:
Phnom Penh is the capital and largest city of Cambodia, located in the south-central part of the country. It is a city of contrasts, in which ancient temples and palaces stand alongside modern skyscrapers and wide boulevards. The rich history and culture here are what make Phnom Penh a great place to start your Cambodian journey. In this video, we highlight 8 of our favorite things to do in and around Phnom Penh.

🔗 PHNOM PENH RESERVATION LINKS, TOURS & OTHER ACTIVITIES 
☑️ National Museum of Cambodia (open from 8 AM to 5 PM): https://www.cambodiamuseum.info/en_information_visitors.html
☑️ Phnom Penh sunset cruise: https://www.getyourguide.com/phnom-penh-l273/phnom-penh-sunset-cruise-with-unlimited-beer-and-drinks-t426772/?partner_id=57UJ5GF&utm_medium=online_publisher&cmp=PhnomPenh_video
☑️ Killing Fields & Genocide Museum tour: https://www.getyourguide.com/phnom-penh-l273/the-killing-field-and-s21-half-day-tour-t442788/?partner_id=57UJ5GF&utm_medium=online_publisher&cmp=PhnomPenh_video
☑️ Phnom Penh Food Tour: https://www.getyourguide.com/phnom-penh-l273/phnom-penh-s-ultimate-food-tour-drinks-tuk-tuk-included-t473701/?partner_id=57UJ5GF&utm_medium=online_publisher&cmp=PhnomPenh_video

😴 WHERE TO STAY IN PHNOM PENH

things to do in phnom penh
things to do in phnom penh cambodia
videos.transcript: What's up guys! We're here in Phnom Penh, the capital city of Cambodia. And in this video, we're going to show you our top things to do here. So hope you enjoy it! Phnom Penh is the capital and largest city of Cambodia, located in the south central part of the country. It's a city of contrasts in which ancient temples and palaces stand along modern skyscrapers and wide boulevards. The rich history and culture here are what make Phnom Penh a great place to start your Cambodian journey. In this video, we highlight eight of our favorite things to do in and around Phnom Penh. These activities are best combined in a 2 to 3 day visit. Make sure to watch until the end where we go over a few travel tips that will help you plan your trip. Now let's dive in! We are Zac and Ine. We make helpful videos about destinations around the world so you can easily plan your own adventures. One of the first things you should do when arriving in Phnom Penh is trying the unique Khmer cuisine. Heavily influenced by the country's geography and history, dishes reflect the abundance of freshwater fish, rice, and tropical fruits. Our top picks include fish amok, which is a fish curry; beef lok lak, a stir-fried beef dish served with rice, vegetables, and a fried egg; Saraman curry, which is a rich coconut curry; and stir-fried noodles or rice. There are tons of great restaurants in the city that serve these dishes at affordable prices. But for a more local experience, head to the night market or one of the food stalls scattered around the city. Sisowath Quay is Phnom Penh's most popular waterfront promenade, stretching through 3 km north from the Royal Palace. Lined with palm trees, restaurants, cafés, and street stalls, it's a great spot for a relaxing stroll. It's also a good place for taking photos of local boats on the river. You can easily combine the walk with the nearby Royal Palace, National Museum, and temples, which we'll talk about in a moment. Buddhism is the predominant religion of Cambodia and Phnom Penh is home to a number of Buddhist temples. We visited Wat Ounalam and Wat Phnom, which are both located in the heart of the city. Wat Ounalam is the most important Buddhist temple in Cambodia and is also home to the head monk in the country. Wat Phnom on the other hand is one of the oldest temples in the city. Both temples are worth visiting for their cultural and historical significance as well as their beautiful architecture. The National Museum is home to a large collection of cultural artifacts from Cambodia's prehistoric times to the post Angkor Period. It's therefore a must-visit for anyone with an interest in the country's history. You can find an array of statues, religious objects, and household utensils made from stone, metal, wood, and ceramics. As a bonus, the exhibit is housed in a traditional building with a beautiful garden, so the setting itself is a work of art. Phnom Penh is a city on the river and one of the most relaxing ways to see it is by boat. Take a sunset cruise for a closer look at local river life, the city's skyline, and important landmarks. Boats depart from the riverfront quay between 4:30 and 5:00 p.m. and generally last about 1.5 hours. Some tours also include free drinks, food, pickup, and an English-speaking guide. You can find more details about the sunset cruise and how to book it via the link in the description below the video. One of Phnom Penh's highlights is undoubtedly the Royal Palace, which is the official residence of the King of Cambodia. Built in the 19th century, the complex is a beautiful example of traditional Khmer architecture. You'll find a number of important buildings here, such as the Silver Pagoda which is home to the Emerald Buddha. Another interesting building to visit is the Throne Hall, which is used for coronations and other Royal ceremonies. If you're interested in learning more about each building, you can hire a local guide at the entrance. But but be aware that the palace is sometimes closed for important events or ceremonies, so come on your first day in the city to avoid missing your opportunity to see it. The Killing Fields are a somber reminder of the terror that the Khmer Rouge regime brought to Cambodia, which ruled the country from 1975 to 1979. During this period, an estimated 1 to 3 million Cambodians were killed. Some died of famine and disease, but others were brutally murdered. At this specific Killing Field, which is found in the outskirts of Phnom Penh, it's estimated that around 17,000 people were assassinated. A visit here is a sobering but essential experience for anyone who wants to understand this tumultuous period in Cambodian history. You can walk around these fields with an audio guide while learning about the regime and its atrocities. In the center, you'll find a stupa which contains 8,000 skulls that have been excavated. It's horrific to see all the mass graves here, because there're just so many. And this one here specifically, like it's a small lot, but there were actually 100 victims of women and children dumped in here. To gain a better understanding of what happened at the Killing Fields, you should combine your visit with the Security Prison S21. This prison was located in a former high school and has now been converted into a museum. Over 17,000 people were tortured between 1975 and 1978. The prison cells are still preserved in their original condition and you can visit them with an audio guide as well. You can also see photographs of the prisoners and leaders of the Khmer Rouge. This genocide museum is a difficult place to visit but it's another important one to learn about this dark period in Cambodian history. We added a link in the description below the video to a tour that combines a visit to this museum along with the Killing Fields. As we promised at the beginning of the video, we'll now share three travel tips for Phnom Penh. First, the official currency of Cambodia is the Cambodian Riel, but US dollars are also widely accepted. Just make sure that your dollars don't have any small tears or marks on them, because they won't be accepted. In a number of places, you can also now pay with a credit card. Second, Phnom Penh is a relatively safe city, but it is important to be aware of your surroundings and take precautions against petty theft. We heard pickpockets do happen, but we didn't experience anything like it ourselves when we visited. Finally, this is a relatively easy city to get around. There are a variety of transportation options available including tuk-tuks, taxis, and buses. To avoid having to bargain over the price, we recommend downloading the Grab App or the Pass App. These are ride hailing Apps and they work for both taxis and tuk-tuks. This was our top selection of things to do in and around Phnom Penh, but of course there are many other activities which are worth checking out if you have more time in the city. We've included a list here on the screen for you. If you're planning your Cambodia trip, we recommend keeping things organized with our interactive travel planner. Find a discount code in the description below. We also included other important links that you'll need for your Phnom Penh trip in the description, such as a full travel guide with where to eat and stay. We'll catch you on the next adventure!

videos.title: 5 Things to Do in Phnom Penh, Cambodia
videos.description:
Phnom Penh is the capital of Cambodia and there are lots of fun things to do including shopping at markets, trying the street food, taking in the nightlife at Bassek Alley, and walking along the Mekong river. You can also learn more about the country's difficult history by visiting the Killing Fields and Genocide Museum. This is our list of 5 things to do when you're visiting Phnom Penh, that we noticed as slow travelers spending time in the area.

#slowtravel #retirementtravel #cambodia #phnompenh
videos.transcript: Hey travelers and welcome to Phnom Penh, the capital of Cambodia. Phnom Penh has a population of about 1 and a half million people and it doesn't seem to be super touristy here. We think it's because most people just bee-line to Siem Reap so that they can visit Angkor Wat. But if you have a little bit of extra time we highly recommend coming down to visit Phnom Penh too. so this is our list of our top five things not to be missed in Phnom Penh, Cambodia that we noticed as slow travelers. Our top five list of things to do in Phnom Penh starts with the markets. There are so many different markets here and they each specialize in different things. Some have more hair and beauty services while others have more food stalls. Some markets have lots of options for fresh meat and fish and others have a more clothing focus. Of all the markets in Phnom Penh, the Russian market is my favorite one for clothing. The Russian market has lots of less expensive clothing and knockoff designer brands. Other markets we love were the Old Market, the Night Market, and the Central Market. Number two on our list of things to do in Phnom Penh, Cambodia is exploring the waterfront area. Here you can stroll along the Mekong River. Many locals come here to have a casual dinner at one of the little picnic type spots set up by the food vendors. This is also where you can catch a boat for a river cruise. We really love people watching here, or traffic watching might be a better term for it, as we were watching commuters board the fairies to take them across the water. It's definitely much easier to get on if you're driving a scooter instead of a car. Next up is the Neak Banh Teuk Park where you can see views of the Independence Monument and the statue of his majesty Preah Bat Samdeck. And also get in some walking. Phnom Penh isn't the most walkable city in the world so people come to this park usually after work when it's a little bit cooler to get their steps in. And everyone walks around the rectangle in the same direction. As we just mentioned Phnom Penh isn't super walkable. There are scooters everywhere and the sidewalks are mostly used for parking. For us, the lack of walkability seemed to be more prominent here than other places we visited as slow travelers in Southeast Asia. So we ended up using the Grab app to take tuk tuks everywhere even if we were just going a short distance. We really like to walk so we didn't love this aspect of the city. On to number four on our list. This afternoon Chris and I are visiting the genocidal museum and the killing fields. We debated a lot as to whether or not we wanted to visit these places because of the emotional toll that it has. But I think that it's really important to understand the history and to know the horrors that greed, hate, and selfishness can bring on the world. We started off by taking a tuk tuk to the Killing Fields Genocidal Center located just outside Phnom Penh. Here we learned how the Khmer Rouge led by Pol Pot committed genocide and murdered over 3 million Cambodians between 1976 and 1978. We then continued our afternoon by visiting the Tuol Sleng Genocide Museum in Phnom Penh itself. This was a tough visit and we left sooner than we planned as all the information was very difficult to take in. I would recommend visiting these places as they're an important part of Cambodian history but be prepared for the emotional impact that it can have. Let's finish off our list of the top five things to do in Phnom Penh with Bassek Alley. This is a fun little street with lots of restaurants ready to cater to tourists. We ordered some delicious food at Don's Mumbai Chaat. Are you going to make me try first? Okay, so they call this samosa pav. It's samosa on a bun. It's got like a mint sauce and chutney. I've been dreaming about this since I saw the menu. It does not disappoint: spicy, savory, a little bit of sweet from the chutney. Excellent! We're slow travelers and normally we spend several weeks or longer in a place. For Phnom Penh our recommendation is to come here for a shorter visit. The lack of walkability really got to us. Though we did enjoy the food, shopping, markets, and waterfront areas. If you've been to Phnom Penh let us know your thoughts of the city in the comments. And that's the end of our video on the top five things to do in Phnom Penh. We hope you're doing well and we'll see you in the next one.

And that's the end of our video on the top five things to do in Phnom Penh

videos.title: 8 Amazing Places You Have to See in Phnom Penh Cambodia!
videos.description:
***Updated sound quality for 2025***

Join me as I explore the must-visit spots in Phnom Penh, Cambodia, offering a perfect mix of history and culture. From golden palaces to bustling markets, this travel vlog highlights the city's unique charm. Experience Cambodia with me as I navigate this popular city!

About Me: I want to share with you beautiful Cambodia through my lens as a Khmer American. Check out my videos here: https://bit.ly/moconutlifeFor more Cambodia adventures, don’t forget to subscribe to get the latest and greatest and I’ll see you in the next video! 

🙏xxLinda

videos.transcript: Punbang is a city of rich history, lively markets, and stunning views. Whether you're here for a quick visit or planning a longer stay, there's so much to explore. From golden palaces and ancient pagotas to bustling street markets and scenic river cruises, Peng offers a perfect mix of history, culture, and adventure. Here are eight must- vvisit spots that you should add to your itinerary. The Central Market is one of Ponumpen's liveliest shopping destinations. Built in the 1930s, its art deco dome makes it one of the most unique looking markets in Southeast Asia. Inside, you'll find everything from handcrafted jewelry and souvenirs to fresh produce and electronics. The wet market section is perfect for food lovers, offering exotic fruits, live seafood, and traditional Cambodian snacks. And if you're feeling adventurous, try a fried tarantula, a scorpion, or a cricket. Now, even if you don't plan to buy anything, it's a great place to people watch and soak in the lively atmosphere. While the US dollar is widely used, paying in local currency can sometimes help you negotiate better prices and get a better deal. And don't forget, keep your belongings secure as the markets can get crowded. What panom is where penumb got its name. Legend has it that in the 1300s, a woman named Lady Ben discovered four Buddha statues floating in the river and built a shrine on this hill to house them. Now, although it's located on a busy roundabout, the gated Pagota is calm and serene, surrounded by lush trees. A short climb up the staircase takes you to the pagota where you'll see intricate carvings, statues, and religious offerings. Inside, visitors light incense and offer prayers, believing it brings good fortune and blessings. You may even see locals releasing small birds as part of a Buddhist merit-making tradition. Here's a tip. After your visit, check out the nearby post office square. It's a quiet historical area with colonial area architecture. There's even a hotel where you can grab some delicious coffee. Check out this video to learn more about it. Located just a short walk from the Royal Palace, the National Museum of Cambodia is a must visit for anyone interested in the country's cultural roots. The museum's red terracotta exterior and peaceful courtyard garden make it a beautiful place to explore even before stepping inside. Surrounded by lush greenery, ponds, and shaded benches, the outdoor space offers a quiet moment to pause and appreciate the architecture and atmosphere. Now, once inside, you'll find centuries old statues, sculptures, and relics that date back to the early Anko period. The collection includes pieces that reflect Cambodian's Hindu and Buddhist influence along with everyday items used by ancient civilizations. As I moved through the exhibits, I felt deeply connected to my heritage. There's something so powerful about standing in front of objects created by our ancestors. many of them beautifully preserved despite the centuries. It reminded me of how rich, complex, and resilient Cambodian culture truly is. Now, photography is allowed, but only with your phones. The Royal Palace is one of Ponen's most famous landmarks and a must visit for anyone exploring the city. Built in 1866, this palace is still home to Cambodia's royal family today. The complex features golden rooftops, intricate Kai architecture, and beautifully manicured gardens. One of the highlights is the silver pagod, named for its floor made of over 5,000 pure silver tiles. Inside, you'll find stunning Buddhist relics, including a life-siz golden Buddha adorned with 9,584 diamonds. While parts of the palace are closed to the public, you can still explore the outer courtyards and temples. At night, the palace is beautifully illuminated, making it one of the best places to capture photos. Next is the river sidewalk, also known as Sisawat Ki. Sisawat Ki runs along the Meong River and is one of Ponain's best places for a walk. The prominade stretches about 3 kilometers, offering river views, street vendors, and plenty of spots to relax. On Saturday and Sunday evenings, the city closes off parts of the road to create Walk Street, a pedestrianonly area. The initiative officially launched in June of 2025 as part of an effort to make public spaces more accessible and vibrant. From 6:00 p.m. to 11:00 p.m., the area fills with food stalls, street performers, and families out enjoying the weekend. There are public restrooms, seating areas, and lights, making it safe and comfortable for everyone. And it's a fun and festive way to experience Pumpang like a local. For an even better view of the city, hop on a sunset cruise along the Meong River. These 1-hour boat rides take you along the water while you'll see the city skyline come to life as the sun sets. Fun fact, the Meong River is home to the largest freshwater stingray ever recorded, discovered in 2022. What's even more fun is you can book an entire boat, grab some of your favorite people for a night of delicious drinks and food. You can bring your own snacks and even grill skewers on board for the full experience. My friends and I even had a karaoke machine to sing the night away. As the sun sets, the Pumpang Night Market transforms into a lively hub of music, shopping, and delicious street food. You'll find stalls selling cheap clothes, accessories, and handmade crafts. But the real highlight, the food. You can try freshly pressed sugarce juice, grilled meats, and the famous papaya salad made fresh at the stalls. And if you're up for a challenge, try balut, which is fertilized duck egg, a Cambodian delicacy. Here's a tip. Locals often eat picnic style on mats at the market, so join in for the full experience. Speaking of food, if you're looking for more must try Cambodian dishes or the best coffee spots in Ponang, check out my other videos for recommendations. If you're looking for another beautiful place to walk, check out the Independence Monument. Built in 1958 to celebrate Cambodia's independence from France, this monument is an important national symbol. The best time to visit is at night when it's beautifully illuminated. The surrounding Independence Park is also a popular place for locals to exercise, dance, or just relax. And if you're up for a walk, head over to Watt Baton Park. Another great spot for an evening stroll. That wraps up my top eight must- visit attractions in Ponump. Which one are you most excited to visit? Let me know in the comments. If you want more recommendations on where to eat or grab a great coffee, check out my other videos. And don't forget to like, share, and subscribe for more travel and food adventures. Clear.

videos.transcript: if you've clicked on this video there's a very good chance that you're thinking of or planning a trip to Phnom Penh Cambodia and I have to say after spending 4 days in this city I have absolutely fallen in love with it it truly is one underrated gem in Asia that is full of amazing food and amazing people that said I would be lying if I had said that I wasn't initially apprehensive about going to Phnom Penh for many reasons including the fact that I do not speak the language fortune favours to prepared so I did a lot of research before my trip and I also learned a thing or two during my time there and in this video I'm going to share absolutely everything I learned from how to get from the airport to the city, the apps you need to navigating the city's dual currency setup and many many more and so without further Ado let's get into this let's start with the best time to go to Phnom Penh from a weather perspective the best time to visit this wonderful city is during the dry season season which takes place in between November to February as the weather is generally cooler and less humid during this time window making it more comfortable for you to explore the city the dry season also coincides with several local festivals which will give you the opportunity to see the city in its liveliest form this includes the Lively bon um Touk I don't know if I got the pronunciation right but I've put the name of the festival right here as well but it's also known as the Water festival and that takes place in November it is one of Cambodia's most vibrant and most celebrated events that marks the end of the rainy season as well as the reversal of the Tonle Sap River flow Expect boat racing, plenty of traditional music dancing and fireworks but what about what to bring in Phnom Penh as Cambodia is a tropical country what you would bring in terms of clothing is fairly straightforward however there are a few things I would recommend you bring and the first one is a tropical country Must Have and that is one of these mini fans this I think cost me around 20 bucks off of Amazon and I have used it in Malaysia, Singapore, Taiwan, Korea and many other places just trust me bring a mini fan you're going to need it The next one is pretty much an essential no matter where you go I in fact pretty much wear it as I'm boarding my plane and that is a comfortable pair of walking shoes because if you are planning to explore a city you best believe that you're going to be doing your steps I must have averaged around 20,000 steps a day easy when I was there and it's best that you do it with a comfortable pair of walking shoes you're also going to be spending a lot of time outdoors and so sunscreens are an absolute standard for me I generally have this roll on one by round lab in my bag for my face and I've got a beach hut one from just my local Australian Pharmacy here that also fits into my bag for the rest of my body such as the arms and neck and legs make sure you bring some so you don't get sunburnt One new item that I specifically got for this trip and in fact will be using for all my upcoming trips is this anti- theft bag it kind of slides into your front like so I don't know if you can see it and it actually fits quite a few things it actually is quite comfortable and has survived a 4-day trip in Phnom Penh with me I got this of Amazon it wasn't expensive I definitely recommend this I also wanted to put this next one in its own section because I think it is an absolutely essential thing not so much to pack but to prepare before your trip and that is an active mobile data connection I personally use my phone a lot when I was in Phnom Penh namely for using Google Maps using Grab, currency conversion and even the occasional translation the most straightforward way to get an active mobile data connection would be obviously to turn on your data roaming however if you're planning to do that definitely check with your Telco provider beforehand to find out what the roaming plans are so that you're not caught with a big Bill at the end of your trip however this might not be practical if you plan to be in Cambodia for a longer period of time and so your next best options would be to purchase a Cambodian esim or physical SIM card so let's talk about those let's first talk about my preferred option and if you're a subscriber you would already know that I'm going to start talking about esims esim is basically a virtual SIM card slot that allows your phone to take on an additional Network whilst at the same time preserving your existing one so that you can continue to receive calls and smses during your trip most phones nowadays come with an esim slot to find out if your phone has one you can either ask your telco provider or check if your phone has an Eid it looks a little something like this in terms of esim options if you're going to Cambodia as a Solo Traveler I personally recommend Airalo which is what I used the data plans start from around 7 Aussie dollars and I can actually get you 15% off if you're a new customer and 10% off if you're an existing customer simply apply the codes that I've placed in my description box under a Airalo when checking out and thank me later however if you're traveling with friends or family or any other situations where you might be sharing a data plan or are just someone who will need a lot of mobile data Holafly is a better option as they offer Unlimited data plan options for Cambodia that start from $18.90 us or around 28 Aussie dollars if you want to give Holafly a go I can also get you 5% off just use the code that's listed under Holafly in my description box if your phone however does not have an esim slot you will need to get a physical SIM card the best place to purchase a Cambodian physical SIM card would be at the Phnom Penh International Airport arrival Halls the SIM cards generally cost anything from $15 to $25 depending on the package you opt for but before you do either the physical or esim card options please make sure that your phone is not locked to your existing Telco provider because if that is the case neither of these options will be for you if it is locked then your only other options would be to turn on the roaming or get a separate mobile device to be used when you're in Cambodia but perhaps one of the most confusing Parts as a tourist or first time traveler to Cambodia is their currency so let's talk about that yes there will be some places that take credit cards namely the huger department stores or some restaurants but cash is the main way you would be paying for yourself as a tourist so I strongly recommend that you have cash on hand Cambodia actually uses two currencies concurrently one is their own native Cambodian Riel AKA KHR but they will also in most cases except USD so if you find it difficult to get some Cambodian reel before your trip just get some USD and you'll be fine hey guys it's editing Steve jumping in again because I realized I forgot to mention that the condition of your US dollar notes matter so if you for example have some US dollar notes that are perhaps a bit old they might not be accepted in Cambodia so just make sure that your bills the US dollar bills that you use are quite clean and new all the menus and things that I looked at when I was in Phnom Penh had pricings in both Cambodian riel and USD in fact they are used so interchangeably that you can pay for something using USD and get change given to you in Cambodian riel if you didn't already have some USD or Cambodian riel on hand however not to worry there are ATM machines or exchange count money exchange counters located at the arrival Halls so we've talked about mobile data connection and money let's now talk entry requirements you will need a visa to enter Cambodia not to worry though as the process to obtain the Visa is fairly straightforward you can either apply for an e Visa online or do it upon arrival in Cambodia though personally I would recommend for you to do it prior to your arrival you just never know how busy the airport is going to be you can apply for the e-Visa via this link which I've also placed in my description box filling in the application form is so straightforward so I won't go through that with you it cost me $36 to process and it took around 3 days to be approved the Visa is single entry and valid for for 30 days don't forget as well as with most other countries that your passport will need to have a 6 month validity if you're entering Cambodia via air you will also need to complete an electronic arrival form this one's free and you can do it via this link within 7 Days of your arrival of course YouTube videos live forever what I've said now is valid as of the time of the writing of this video it is always pertinent for you to double check this information with the official immigration website at the time of your trip okay you've arrived in Phnom Penh let's now talk about how to get you from the airport to the city the Phnom Penh international airport is located roughly 10 km from the city it will take you around 30 minutes or so to get there and there are a few options to consider the most straightforward one would be to catch a cab you can get a cab once you leave the arrival Halls of the Phnom Penh International Airport however remember how we spoke about the eims earlier I would recommend to activate your esim upon arrival in Phnom Penh and book the cab to your accommodation via Grab instead the reason for this apart from potential language barriers is that the price to take you from the airport to your accommodation will be fixed it is fairly cheap to catch a cab I mean it's going to cost you anything from $9 to a maximum of $15 to get a cab from the airport to your accommodation but if you're looking for a slightly cheaper option I would recommend grabbing a Tuk Tuk you can also book a Tuk Tuk via the grab app just a point to note that if you do have a lot of luggage catching a Tuk Tuk is probably not practical but whether you get a grab or Tuk Tuk you will need to go to the grab waiting area to hop on your right it is fairly straightforward to locate the waiting area I mean you can't miss it it is this huge green sign that says grab and you will see it across the parking area once you've exited the arrival Halls depending on where you're staying by the way your hotel May even offer pickup services so it might be worthwhile checking with your accommodation before your trip if this comes with your accommodation speaking of accommodation one of the most frequently asked questions by visitors to pane is where to stay by far the most popular area to stay in Phnom Penh is Riverside it is located along Mekong river and is home to a variety of accommodation options from luxury hotels to Cozy guest houses that cater to different budgets the key selling point of Riverside is that it is within walking distance to popular attraction spots such as the Royal Palace, the National Museum and the markets it is a great choice if you love to be in the center of action as the dining nightlife options here are also expansive and so if you don't mind the hustle and bustle and are traveling to Phnom Penh for the first time I would recommend staying in this area in terms of specifically where I ended up booking a room at the plantation Urban Resort and Spa which is located just behind the royal palace and I did this for a few reasons first of all look at how stunning and high end this hotel looks the resort comprises two beautiful outdoor pools the spa there's also a free daily breakfast that comes with it the breakfast nook overlooks one of the main outdoor pools but by far the best part of this all is my room here cost just 64 Singapore dollars a night and so if you were to ask me for my honest opinion I would say don't think anymore this is the place you you should be staying at if you're visiting Phnom Penh now that you're all checked in you are ready to explore the city so let's now talk about how you're going to be getting around and if you're staying in the Riverside area most of the popular tourist attraction spots will be within walking distance the best way to get around if you're not into walking in tropical weather would be to catch a cab it is so cheap it would generally cost you around $1 to $5 us to get around however much like the ride from the airport to the city I would recommend booking your rides via grab or even consider using their local version of grab which I believe is called Pass App catching a cap is going to be the best way to get around especially if you're traveling with family or a group of people however if you're traveling by yourself or even just with another plus one I highly recommend getting around using their Tuk tuks it is an experience that must be had if you're in pane however if you are writing a Tuk Tuk do make note to be very careful about your belongings and that leads quite nicely in into this next section where we talk about safety now I want to preface this section by saying that I met some of the nicest people when I was in penom and I had the best time and also nothing happened to me while I was there however my pre-trip research and also the receptionist at my accommodation they advised me to be really careful about my belongings while I was navigating myself around the city as theft targeting foreigners are especially prevalent on sidewalks on motorcycles and tuk tuks and so apart from being really careful about your belongings I would also recommend again that anti- theft bag that I showed you earlier having said all of that though I would imagine that this is a common practice no matter where you go to but I just thought that I would put this in as well but what should I be checking out Steve where should we go, what can you do in Phnom Penh? I will be uploading another video next week that showcases all the places I went to when I was in Phnom Penh I have seen some amazing things from beautiful temples to trying out some absolutely delicious Cuisine I'm Steve from the phatlife project by the way and my channel is all about creating PHAT travel guides just like this one that you're watching now to help you simplify your travel planning so if that is your Vibe I strongly recommend clicking on that subscribe button so that we can go on more fat travel adventures together don't forget to also like and comment if you found value in this video it really does help in appeasing those YouTube algorithm Gods but more importantantly it just makes my day as a content creator and if you got to this point of the video thank you so much for watching as always I truly appreciate it I do hope that you have a fantastic day ahead or that you've already had a good day as always I will see you very soon in the next video ciao!!
